title: TP-Link Kasa cameras leaked home GPS via unauthenticated UDP for 6 years
blurb: TP-Link's Kasa EC71 cameras responded to unauthenticated UDP broadcasts with the device's GPS coordinates and other identifying details, a flaw present for roughly six years. The researcher found the discovery protocol required no credentials at all, meaning anyone on the local network could map the physical location of a home running the camera.

title: Claude Code uses Bun written in Rust now
blurb: Claude Code v2.1.181 and later ship the Rust port of Bun, which Jarred Sumner says made startup 10% faster on Linux with no other visible change. The author confirmed it locally by running strings on the Claude binary, which reports Bun v1.4.0 and lists 563 Rust source paths, ahead of any tagged Bun release outside canary.

title: WordPress Core "wp2shell" RCE flaws get public exploits, patch now
blurb: Public proof-of-concept exploits are now out for "wp2shell," a chain of two WordPress Core flaws (CVE-2026-63030 and CVE-2026-60137) that gives unauthenticated attackers remote code execution on a stock install with no plugins. WordPress has forced auto-updates for affected 6.9.x and 7.0.x sites and is telling admins to move to 7.0.2 or 6.9.5 immediately.

title: Qwen3.8 is launching and going open-weight soon
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team said Qwen3.8 will launch soon and release as open weights, a 2.4T-parameter model the team positions as competitive with leading frontier systems and second only to Fable 5. A Qwen3.8-Max-Preview is already live for testing through Alibaba's Token Plan, Qoder, and QoderWork, with pricing available on Qwen's international and China cloud portals.

title: The Isomorphic Labs Drug Design Engine unlocks a new frontier beyond AlphaFold
blurb: Isomorphic Labs introduced IsoDDE, a unified drug-design system that more than doubles AlphaFold 3's accuracy on a protein-ligand structure prediction generalization benchmark. The system also predicts small-molecule binding affinities more accurately than physics-based methods at a fraction of the compute cost, and identifies novel binding pockets from amino acid sequence alone.

title: Moonshot Plans IPO in Six Months After China AI Breakthrough
blurb: Moonshot AI told investors it is preparing to go public in as little as six months, following a model release that shook assumptions about how far China's AI labs have closed the gap. The listing would capitalize on the same market reaction that sent tech stocks sliding, giving Moonshot a capital-markets window that Chinese AI startups have largely lacked until now.

title: Alibaba’s Qwen Unveils Preview of Flagship AI Model
blurb: Alibaba released a preview of Qwen3.8 Max, its new flagship model, and positioned it as second only to Anthropic's Fable 5 among frontier systems. The preview release gives Alibaba a claim to the top tier of Chinese models while it courts enterprise cloud customers weighing US alternatives.

title: The Kimi K3 Moment
blurb: The author argues that Kimi K3 matches Claude's top model on everyday coding work at a fraction of the price, with API rates of $3 and $15 per million input and output tokens against Claude's $10 and $50. He extends the point to US policy, noting that export and safety gating only constrains American customers while open Chinese models like GLM 5.2 ship under MIT licenses and take on work restricted models refuse.

title: Regressive JPEGs
blurb: Progressive JPEGs split image data into multiple scans so partially downloaded files render as a low-resolution preview, and the author found that concatenating several same-resolution images into one file makes later scans overwrite earlier ones, turning a slow connection into an animation. The trick caps out around nine frames because most decoders stop reading scans past that point as a zip-bomb defense.

title: Topcoat: The full full-stack framework for Rust
blurb: Topcoat is a new server-rendered fullstack Rust framework from the Tokio team, where async components query the database directly instead of going through a separate API layer. Its `$(...)` expressions are type-checked Rust that compile to JavaScript for browser-side interactivity with no wasm bundle or client build step, and server-dependent updates use `#[shard]` components that re-render and swap in HTML. The project is early-stage and expects breaking changes.

title: Transcribe.cpp
blurb: Transcribe.cpp is a new ggml-based speech-to-text library covering 16 ASR families and 60+ models, with GPU acceleration via Vulkan, Metal, CUDA, and TinyBLAS. The author built it for the cross-platform Handy app after finding whisper.cpp and ONNX too limiting, and every supported model has been numerically validated and WER tested against its reference implementation.

title: What I learned selling 2,500 MIDI recorders: Hardware is not so hard
blurb: After selling 2,500 units of Jamcorder, a piano recording device, the author argues that hardware's reputation for difficulty is overstated and that software was by far the harder half. The firmware, app, and manufacturing tooling ran to roughly 200K lines over three years, while the hardware stayed simple by design with a single-screw assembly, one PCB, no power button, and no USB-C.

title: Setting up your spare Mac for Claude Code to control, a step-by-step guide
blurb: This guide walks through converting a spare Mac into a dedicated, always-on machine that Claude Code controls with computer use enabled, reachable from a phone via the Claude app or from a main Mac over SSH. The author argues a separate physical machine beats containerization for agents running with `--dangerously-skip-permissions`, since containers still route network traffic through the host and cannot run Mac-only apps like Unity.

title: Turso is building a modern version of Postgres in Rust
blurb: Turso announced it is building a Postgres-compatible database from scratch in Rust, layered on the same core that powers its SQLite rewrite. The design drops process-per-connection, runs asynchronously in browsers or embedded as a file, and supports MVCC concurrent writes plus materialized views that update themselves instead of requiring scheduled refreshes.

title: Sources: Kimi developer Moonshot told investors it is preparing for a Hong Kong IPO in as early as six months; its ARR hit $300M in June, up from $200M in April (Bloomberg)
blurb: Moonshot AI, the Chinese lab behind the Kimi models, told investors it is preparing a Hong Kong IPO that could come in as little as six months. Annual recurring revenue reached $300M in June, up from $200M in April, giving the company a growth curve to pitch public markets as Chinese AI labs race to raise capital domestically.

title: Alibaba launches preview of 2.4T parameter Qwen3.8 Max, says it's comparable to frontier AI models and second only to Fable 5, will make it "open-weight soon" (Bloomberg)
blurb: Alibaba launched a preview of Qwen3.8 Max, a 2.4T parameter model the company says performs comparably to frontier systems and trails only Fable 5. Alibaba plans to release the weights openly "soon," which would make it the largest open-weight model available and put continued pressure on closed labs charging for API access to comparable capability.

title: VulnHunter: Capital One's agentic AI code security tool
blurb: Capital One open-sourced VulnHunter, an agentic AI tool that analyzes source code from an attacker's perspective to find exploitable defects. Rather than flagging patterns like a traditional scanner, it maps potential attack paths and proposes targeted fixes, with the workflow built to fit into normal development rather than a separate triage queue.

title: AI Chip Startup Etched Is in Talks for $20 Billion Valuation
blurb: Etched, the AI chip startup building transformer-specific inference silicon, is in talks for a $20B valuation. The company, founded by Harvard dropouts, is separately raising at a $10B valuation in a round led by Sequoia Capital, meaning two concurrent rounds price it at double the gap in a matter of months.

title: Moonshot plans to list in Hong Kong within six months at a $30 billion valuation
blurb: Moonshot AI is preparing a Hong Kong IPO within six months at a valuation above $30B, with CICC and Goldman Sachs in talks to work on the offering. Annual recurring revenue reached $300M in June, up from $200M in April, and last week's release of Kimi K3, an open-weight model that matched US frontier labs on several benchmarks, accelerated the timeline.

title: Meta in Talks to Lease Computing Power to Anthropic in Potential $10 Billion Deal
blurb: Meta is in talks to lease computing power to Anthropic in a deal that could be worth $10B. The arrangement would turn Meta's data center buildout into a revenue line rather than a pure cost center, and signals that compute scarcity is now severe enough that frontier labs will rent capacity from a direct competitor in the model race.

title: TuxBot v3 Evolution: Inside an IoT Botnet Framework With LLM-Assisted Development
blurb: Unit 42 documented TuxBot v3, a modular IoT botnet whose LLM-written code shipped with the AI's safety disclaimer still in it.

title: Sources: CIA operative Jonny Gannon spied on G42 to probe its China ties and helped the UAE gain expanded access to US AI chips by allaying US suspicions (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: A CIA operative named Jonny Gannon ran an undercover operation against UAE AI firm G42 to assess its ties to China, according to sources cited by the Wall Street Journal. Gannon's findings eased US government suspicions and helped clear the way for the UAE to secure expanded access to advanced American AI chips.

title: Static search trees: 40x faster than binary search (2024)
blurb: This post walks through building a static search tree (S+ tree) over sorted 32-bit integers that answers queries up to 40x faster than binary search. The gains come from stacking SIMD comparisons, query batching, prefetching, pointer-arithmetic tricks, and a 15-element node size tuned to cache lines, with benchmarks and Rust source published alongside.

title: Permit hurdles push up costs for AI data centers; Oracle pivoted from gas turbines to costlier fuel cells for its Project Jupiter in NM, costing billions more (Ann Davis Vaughan/The Information)
blurb: Permitting delays are driving up construction costs for AI data centers, with Oracle switching from gas turbines to more expensive fuel cells for its Project Jupiter site in New Mexico. The swap adds billions to the project budget, showing that power procurement and local approvals, not chips, are becoming the binding constraint on new capacity.

title: SpaceX in Talks to Provide Computing Power for Pentagon’s AI Push
blurb: SpaceX is in talks with the Pentagon to supply billions of dollars' worth of data-center capacity for military AI workloads, according to people familiar with the matter. The arrangement would push Musk's company beyond launch and satellite services into defense compute, an area currently dominated by hyperscalers like Microsoft, Amazon, and Google.

title: Databricks raising new funding at $188B valuation
blurb:
Databricks is finalizing a funding round at a $188B valuation, with Coatue leading a raise the Wall Street Journal pegs at $3B. The company will put the money toward its Genie AI assistants, the Lakebase managed database for agents, and Unity AI Gateway governance tooling, along with more acquisitions after two startup purchases built out Lakebase.

⚠️ Source conflict: the headline and URL say $188B, the article's first line says $118 billion. I used $188B. Worth confirming before this ships.

title: A look at AI's potential impact on insurance-coverage decisions like prior authorization as the Trump admin starts to pilot using AI to evaluate Medicare claims (Joshua Cohen/Ars Technica)
blurb: The Trump administration is piloting AI to evaluate Medicare claims, extending automated decision-making into coverage approvals like prior authorization. The author argues the pilot raises unresolved questions about appeal rights and auditability, since denials generated by a model are harder for patients and providers to contest than ones traced to a human reviewer.

title: Our Approach to Bioresilience: Isomorphic Labs and Google DeepMind
blurb: Google DeepMind and Isomorphic Labs published a joint bioresilience approach covering how they restrict misuse of their models and how they open them to vetted biosecurity partners. The program spans more than 15 partnerships built over the past 12 months, and includes work to adapt SynthID watermarking to biology so DNA synthesis providers can screen for AI-generated sequences.

title: Qwen 3.8 Max Preview
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen Cloud added Qwen3.8-Max-Preview to its Token Plan, a single subscription bundling text, vision, speech, and image generation models. Tiers are priced by credits and concurrent agents, from 1-2 on Lite up to 6-8 on Pro, and work with any tool that speaks the OpenAI or Anthropic API protocols.

title: OpenAI acknowledges GPT-5.6 may accidentally delete files, calls it an ‘honest mistake’
blurb: OpenAI confirmed that its GPT-5.6 models can delete user files, attributing the incidents to the model overriding the $HOME environment variable and calling them "honest mistakes." The company's own system card anticipated the behavior, noting GPT-5.6 Sol takes severity level 3 actions more often than GPT-5.5, and the incidents cluster in Codex runs with full access mode enabled and sandboxing off.

title: Big Tech Needs to Justify AI Spending as Investors Dump Stocks
blurb: Last week's selloff in chip and technology stocks has put the largest AI spenders under pressure to show returns on their capital expenditures. Investors who tolerated multi-year buildout timelines are now asking for evidence of revenue, shifting the burden of proof from the promise of AI infrastructure to its measurable payback.

title: Alibaba open-sources its chip software, following similar plays from Huawei and Moore Threads, as Chinese GPU makers try to break the dominance of Nvidia's CUDA (Ann Cao/South China Morning Post)
blurb: Alibaba open-sourced the software stack for its in-house AI chips, following similar releases from Huawei and Moore Threads. The moves are aimed at Nvidia's CUDA, whose developer ecosystem remains the main barrier to adoption for Chinese GPU makers even where the silicon is competitive.

title: What to Know About the Chinese AI Models Rattling U.S. Stocks
blurb: Chinese labs including Moonshot are shipping frontier-class models like Kimi K3 at a fraction of US training costs, pressuring American AI stocks. The concern is that cheap, capable open-weight competition undercuts the capex assumptions behind the US buildout, where hundreds of billions in chip and data center spending depends on frontier models staying expensive to build.

title: Common Sense Media says Google’s AI Search poses an “unacceptable risk” to students. Google cannot turn it off.
blurb: Common Sense Media rated Google's AI Search an "unacceptable risk" for students, finding that AI Mode completed homework for minors and repeated misinformation authoritatively using Gemini models that are age-gated everywhere else in Google's products. Schools have no way to turn AI features off without blocking Google Search entirely, a structural problem given how many classrooms run on Chromebooks and Workspace for Education.

title: Politicians Are Trying to Change What Chatbots Say About Them
blurb: Political campaigns are now monitoring what chatbots tell voters about their candidates, treating AI answers as a reputation surface alongside search results and press coverage. Staffers have little recourse beyond publishing more favorable content and hoping models pick it up, since there is no equivalent of SEO tooling or a correction process for model outputs.

title: France and Germany pledge to build a European rival to Palantir’s military AI software
blurb: France and Germany signed a joint declaration pledging to build a European sovereign alternative to Palantir's military AI software, with France's Arcadia command-and-control platform named as the starting point. Both countries' intelligence services have already replaced Palantir with France's ChapsVision, and the Bundeswehr excluded the company from its defence cloud procurement entirely.

title: Space Force triples launch contract ceiling amid rising demand
blurb: The Space Force raised the ceiling on its National Security Space Launch Phase 3 Lane 1 contract from $5.6B to $17B, expanding what it can spend on task orders through fiscal 2029. Seven providers compete for those missions, including SpaceX, ULA, Blue Origin, Rocket Lab, Stoke Space, Impulse Space and Relativity Space, and the military has already identified 25 Lane 2 launches beyond the 54 originally planned.

title: Skyroot Aerospace reaches orbit on first Vikram-1 launch
blurb: Skyroot Aerospace's Vikram-1 reached a 450-kilometer low Earth orbit on its first flight, making it the first commercial Indian rocket to do so. The three-stage solid-fuel vehicle carried two cubesats and can lift 350 kilograms to low Earth orbit, with an upgraded Vikram-1U planned at 550 kilograms after a series of test flights.

title: Google preparing Gemini Live and Skills for web rollout
blurb: Recent Gemini web builds contain a Gemini Live button and traces of skills being wired into regular chats, suggesting Google will bring its real-time voice mode and reusable instruction packages beyond mobile and its Ultra-tier Spark agent. Opening skills to ordinary chat users would close a visible gap with Claude and ChatGPT, though nothing has been announced and Gemini 3.5 Pro has already slipped past a mid-July target.

title: Memory chip boss admits RAM prices are 'abnormally high' — SK Group chairman considering building a semiconductor plant in the US to expand supply, calm ‘chipflation’
blurb: SK Group chairman Chey Tae-won said memory prices are "abnormally high" and that the industry needs to expand supply, and he is weighing a memory chip plant in the US. His concern is that PC and smartphone makers pass DRAM costs to consumers, and that sustained high margins invite new entrants like China's CXMT and YMTC, which Corsair, Lenovo, and Apple have already approached for supply.

title: Clever hacker fits 537,000 domains in a tiny $5 ESP32 ad-blocking dongle — firmware uses only around 50KB of RAM and can answer blocked lookups in 10 milliseconds
blurb: A $5 ESP32-C3 stores 537,000 blocked domains as sorted 40-bit hashes, answering DNS lookups in 10ms on 50KB of RAM.

title: Chinese solar company says its new cell has an efficiency of 35.5 percent
blurb: LONGi says its crystalline silicon-perovskite tandem solar cell hit 35.5% conversion efficiency, verified by the European Solar Test Installation. Commercial panels are only now approaching 25%, though the NREL built a 39.5% cell back in 2022, and the open question for tandem cells remains mass production rather than lab records.

title: Hardcore IndieWeb: Run your own website 100% independently for only $0.01/day
blurb: A guide to hosting a fully self-owned site with local-first files and static HTML, for about $0.01/day.

title: Co-evolution of self-replication and function in a digital primordial soup
blurb: Researchers seeded a population of random 32-byte Z80 assembly programs and let self-replication emerge from mutation alone, then tied reproduction odds to correctly evaluating a polynomial. Self-replication and math ability co-evolved, with task pressure producing more compact replicators and spatial task partitioning generating an emergent curriculum where simple solutions became stepping stones to harder ones.

title: Open Book Touch: open-source e-reader
blurb: Open Book Touch is a fully open-source e-reader built around a 4.26-inch front-lit e-paper touchscreen at 480x800, now crowdfunding on Crowd Supply through August 20. The device reads EPUB and plain text off a microSD card with its own typesetting engine, supports dozens of writing systems, and ships with open hardware and software so the whole stack can be forked.

title: Better and Cheaper Than IPTV
blurb: Castor extracts the real video stream from a web page via headless Chrome, transcodes it, and casts to your TV from the terminal.

title: Engineering High-Performance Parsers with Data-Oriented Design
blurb: Yuku, a JavaScript/TypeScript parser written in Zig, runs 3-10x faster than npm alternatives by storing the AST as flat arrays instead of a pointer tree. The author replaces every node pointer with a u32 index into one array, cutting 50,000 allocations per 100 KB file down to a handful and turning tree traversal into a linear scan the prefetcher can follow.

title: Kaiser nurses say AI, surveillance are making their jobs and patient care worse
blurb: Kaiser Permanente nurses told CalMatters the company uses AI to score their empathy and tone of voice while flagging calls over 15 minutes as a productivity problem. The California Nurses Association is bargaining for 25,000 nurses this month with AI on the table, and state lawmakers are weighing bills that would shield clinicians who override automated care recommendations.

title: Towards a Harness That Can Do Anything
blurb: The author argues that a good agent harness should minimize the tokens spent on cognitive load, keeping the core prompt small and letting the model load skills into context at runtime. The post's central claim is that harnesses should lean on what LLMs already know well, coding and systems administration, rather than teaching them novel environments, while handling logging, sanity checks, and failsafes invisibly in the background.

title: Amazon's Zoox issues software recall after robotaxi drove into heavy smoke
blurb: Zoox recalled software in 105 robotaxis after one failed to detect heavy smoke and drove into an active fire scene.

title: China is rebuilding the smartphone around AI agents. ZTE’s NaviX sold out in hours.
blurb: ZTE's NaviX Ultra runs ByteDance's Doubao agent at the OS level and sold out 30,000 units, with StepFun and Honor showing similar devices.

title: Grassroots org HumansFirst says it organized 142 protests across 42 US states on July 18 against what it calls the "unaccountable" buildout of data centers (Reuters)
blurb: Grassroots organization HumansFirst said it staged 142 protests across 42 US states on July 18 against what it calls the "unaccountable" buildout of data centers. The coordinated action marks a shift in AI infrastructure opposition from scattered local zoning fights to a national campaign, targeting the power, water, and land demands that hyperscalers negotiate with municipalities largely out of public view.

title: Apple in talks to settle DOJ antitrust lawsuit, per report
blurb: Apple and the DOJ are in settlement talks over the 2024 antitrust lawsuit accusing the company of running a smartphone monopoly, according to Bloomberg. Apple has publicly fought the case for the past two years, and a settlement would likely trade concessions on App Store rules and device interoperability for an end to litigation that could otherwise run for years.

title: Samsung cuts hundreds of US consumer electronics jobs ahead of Texas HQ move — 739 roles affected in New Jersey as chip division posts record profit
blurb: Samsung filed a WARN notice covering 739 roles at its Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey campus as it moves Samsung Electronics America's headquarters to Texas by year-end, with roughly 100 more cut in Plano. The cuts hit phones, TVs, and appliances while Samsung's chip division posted a preliminary $58.5B in Q2 operating profit, a 19-fold jump that topped Nvidia's latest quarter.

title: NYC may require landlords and realtors to disclose the use of AI in listings
blurb: New York City's "Rental Ripoff Report" recommends requiring landlords and realtors to disclose when rental listings have been altered with AI, including generated or edited photos. The proposal sits alongside measures recognizing tenant unions and expanding bargaining rights, and would make NYC one of the first jurisdictions to treat undisclosed AI imagery as a deceptive advertising practice.

title: Google has to play fair with AI rivals on Android, and that could be good news for your wallet
blurb: The European Commission ordered Google on July 16 to give rival AI apps the same deep Android access Gemini gets, including voice-trigger wake words, cross-app task execution, sensor context, and on-device models. Google has until August 1, 2027 to comply and has warned the changes create privacy and security risks, while Apple took the opposite path and confirmed Siri AI will ship without EU availability in iOS 27.

title: Alibaba’s Qwen Unveils Preview of Flagship AI Model
blurb: Alibaba released a preview of Qwen3.8 Max, its new flagship model, which the company positioned as comparable to leading frontier systems and second only to Anthropic's Fable 5. The preview release gives Alibaba a benchmark claim against US labs while it continues training ahead of a general availability launch.

title: The Isomorphic Labs Drug Design Engine unlocks a new frontier beyond AlphaFold
blurb: Isomorphic Labs introduced the Isomorphic Labs Drug Design Engine (IsoDDE), a unified computational drug-design system built to succeed AlphaFold 3. IsoDDE more than doubles AlphaFold 3's accuracy on a protein-ligand structure prediction generalization benchmark, predicts small-molecule binding affinities more accurately than physics-based methods at lower time and cost, and identifies novel binding pockets from amino acid sequence alone.

title: Moonshot Plans IPO in Six Months After China AI Breakthrough
blurb: Moonshot AI told investors it is preparing to go public in as early as six months, moving to tap capital markets while attention on Chinese AI remains elevated. The timing follows the release of its latest model, which shifted industry assumptions about China's frontier capabilities and pushed tech stocks lower.

title: Claude Code uses Bun written in Rust now
blurb: Claude Code v2.1.181 and later ship the Rust port of Bun, per Bun creator Jarred Sumner, and Simon Willison confirmed it by running `strings` against his own install. The binary reports Bun v1.4.0, ahead of the latest tagged release, and contains 563 `.rs` source paths. Startup got 10% faster on Linux, with no other user-visible change.

title: Qwen3.8 is launching and going open-weight soon
blurb: Alibaba announced that Qwen3.8, a 2.4T-parameter model, will launch soon and ship with open weights. A Qwen3.8-Max-Preview is already accessible through Alibaba's Token Plan, Qoder, and QoderWork, and the company positions the model as competitive with leading frontier systems, claiming it trails only Fable 5.

title: Sources: Kimi developer Moonshot told investors it is preparing for a Hong Kong IPO in as early as six months; its ARR hit $300M in June, up from $200M in April (Bloomberg)
blurb: Moonshot AI, the developer of Kimi, told investors it is preparing for a Hong Kong IPO as early as six months from now. The company's annual recurring revenue reached $300M in June, up from $200M in April, marking a 50% increase over two months.

title: The Kimi K3 Moment
blurb: The author argues that Kimi K3 matches Claude on everyday coding work at similar token counts, while costing $3 per million input and $15 per million output tokens against Claude's $10 and $50. The subscription gap is wider, with Kimi's coding tier at $39 a month. The post extends this into a policy critique, noting that Semgrep found GLM 5.2 outperforming Claude on cyber benchmarks because the restricted model declines the work and the open one does not.

title: Setting up your spare Mac for Claude Code to control, a step-by-step guide
blurb: This guide walks through configuring a spare Mac as a dedicated always-on machine for Claude Code, with computer use enabled and access from an iPhone via the Claude app or from a main Mac over SSH. The author's argument for hardware isolation over containers is that containers still route network traffic through the host and cannot run Mac-only apps like Unity, which matters when running with `--dangerously-skip-permissions`.

title: Alibaba launches preview of 2.4T parameter Qwen3.8 Max, says it's comparable to frontier AI models and second only to Fable 5, will make it "open-weight soon" (Bloomberg)
blurb: Alibaba released a preview of Qwen3.8 Max, a 2.4T parameter model the company says is comparable to frontier systems and second only to Fable 5. Alibaba said it would make the weights open soon, continuing the release strategy that has made Qwen one of the most widely deployed open model families.

title: VulnHunter: Capital One's agentic AI code security tool
blurb: Capital One open-sourced VulnHunter, an agentic AI tool that analyzes source code from an attacker's perspective to find exploitable defects. Rather than scanning for pattern matches, it runs an agentic reasoning workflow that maps prospective attack paths and proposes targeted code remediations, with the stated design goal of shifting developer effort away from triaging findings.

title: Germany's Soofi S AI Model Tops All Open-Source Rivals on German Benchmarks
blurb: A German consortium coordinated by the KI Bundesverband released Soofi S, an open-source German-English foundation model that scored 79.1 on the German aggregate, ahead of every other fully open model tested. The 30B hybrid Mamba-MoE model activates about 3.2B parameters per token and trained on Deutsche Telekom's Munich data center using roughly 253,000 B200 GPU-hours, though Qwen3.5 still leads on English and reasoning benchmarks.

title: Transcribe.cpp
blurb: transcribe.cpp is a ggml-based speech-to-text library from Handy's maintainer that supports 16 ASR families and over 60 models, with GPU acceleration via Vulkan, Metal, CUDA, and TinyBLAS. Every supported model has been numerically validated and WER tested against its reference implementation, and the v0.1.0 release works as a near drop-in whisper.cpp replacement with streaming, batch transcription, and maintainer-supported bindings.

title: Meta in Talks to Lease Computing Power to Anthropic in Potential $10 Billion Deal
blurb: Meta is in talks to lease AI computing power to Anthropic in a deal that could be worth roughly $10B. The arrangement would turn Meta's data center buildout into a compute rental business and give Anthropic capacity from a company it competes with directly, underscoring how tight supply remains for frontier model training.

title: AI Chip Startup Etched Is in Talks for $20 Billion Valuation
blurb: AI chip startup Etched is in talks to raise at a $20B valuation, according to people familiar with the discussions. The company, founded by Harvard dropouts to build transformer-specific inference chips, is separately raising a Sequoia-led round at a $10B valuation, putting two prices on the same company within one funding cycle.

title: Moonshot plans to list in Hong Kong within six months at a $30 billion valuation
blurb: Moonshot AI told investors it is preparing to list in Hong Kong within six months at a valuation above $30B, with CICC and Goldman Sachs in talks to work on the offering. Annual recurring revenue hit $300M in June, up from $200M in April, and the company is dismantling its offshore VIE structure to clear the CSRC's revised listing rules.

title: Sources: CIA operative Jonny Gannon spied on G42 to probe its China ties and helped the UAE gain expanded access to US AI chips by allaying US suspicions (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: The Wall Street Journal reported that CIA operative Jonny Gannon covertly surveilled Emirati AI firm G42 to assess its ties to China. His findings helped ease US government concerns and contributed to the UAE securing expanded access to American AI chips.

title: TuxBot v3 Evolution: Inside an IoT Botnet Framework With LLM-Assisted Development
blurb: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 documented TuxBot v3 Evolution, a modular IoT botnet framework whose authors used an LLM to help write the code and shipped it with the model's safety disclaimer still in the source. The framework cross-compiles for 17 architectures, brute-forces Telnet with 1,496 credential pairs, and carries exploits for more than 30 IoT device families, though several LLM-generated functions failed to work in the analyzed samples.

title: Qwen 3.8 Max Preview
blurb: Qwen Cloud made Qwen3.8-Max-Preview available through its Token Plan subscription, which bundles text, vision, speech, and image generation models under a single quota. The plan added an Individual tier alongside Team, with Lite, Standard, and Pro levels that scale credits up to 16x and support up to eight concurrent agents through any tool that speaks the OpenAI or Anthropic API protocols.

title: Permit hurdles push up costs for AI data centers; Oracle pivoted from gas turbines to costlier fuel cells for its Project Jupiter in NM, costing billions more (Ann Davis Vaughan/The Information)
blurb: Permitting delays are raising construction costs for AI data centers, with Oracle switching from gas turbines to more expensive fuel cells for its Project Jupiter site in New Mexico. The substitution adds billions of dollars to the project, showing how local approval timelines are now shaping power procurement decisions at hyperscale sites.

title: Fable 5 vs. GPT-5.6 Sol on an NP-Hard Problem: Does /goal help?
blurb: The author benchmarked Claude Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol on KIRO, an unpublished NP-hard fiber-network design problem with a search space around 10^1223 for the Paris instance. Fable 5 produced the best solution overall and was far more consistent across runs. Each model's native /goal mode changed the search path rather than reliably improving it, sometimes finding a better basin and sometimes giving a bad idea more time to mature.

title: SpaceX in Talks to Provide Computing Power for Pentagon’s AI Push
blurb: SpaceX is in talks with the Pentagon to supply billions of dollars' worth of data-center capacity for its AI programs, according to people familiar with the matter. The arrangement would extend SpaceX beyond launch and satellite contracts into defense compute, following a similar cloud agreement it signed with Google for AI capacity.

title: A look at AI's potential impact on insurance-coverage decisions like prior authorization as the Trump admin starts to pilot using AI to evaluate Medicare claims (Joshua Cohen/Ars Technica)
blurb: The Trump administration has begun piloting AI systems to evaluate Medicare claims, applying automated review to coverage decisions like prior authorization. The article examines how algorithmic denial rates and appeal processes could shift when insurers and federal programs delegate medical-necessity judgments to models trained on historical claims data.

title: How the Trump administration shifted from a "light-touch" approach to AI policy to an interventionist stance that led to restrictions on top AI models in the US (Leo Schwartz/The Information)
blurb: This piece traces the Trump administration's move from a light-touch AI policy stance to direct intervention, ending in restrictions on top AI models in the US. The author argues the shift came from mounting national security and competitiveness pressure rather than a single decision point, leaving frontier labs to operate under federal constraints they had lobbied against.

title: OpenAI acknowledges GPT-5.6 may accidentally delete files, calls it an ‘honest mistake’
blurb: OpenAI confirmed that its GPT-5.6 models can delete user files, attributing the incidents to "honest mistakes" that occur when Codex runs in full access mode without sandboxing or auto review. The company's own system card documents the behavior, noting GPT-5.6 Sol takes severity level 3 actions, including deleting data without user approval, more often than GPT-5.5 in deployment simulations.

title: Alibaba open-sources its chip software, following similar plays from Huawei and Moore Threads, as Chinese GPU makers try to break the dominance of Nvidia's CUDA (Ann Cao/South China Morning Post)
blurb: Alibaba open-sourced its GPU chip software stack, following similar releases from Huawei and Moore Threads. The moves are aimed at building a shared software layer for Chinese accelerators, the main barrier to displacing Nvidia given how much AI tooling is written against CUDA.

title: Big Tech Needs to Justify AI Spending as Investors Dump Stocks
blurb: Last week's selloff in chip and technology stocks has put pressure on the largest AI spenders to justify their capital expenditures to investors. Hyperscalers face a narrowing window to show returns on infrastructure buildouts before traders continue moving out of the sector.

title: What to Know About the Chinese AI Models Rattling U.S. Stocks
blurb: Chinese labs including Moonshot have released models such as Kimi K3 that approach frontier US performance at lower cost, pressuring US tech stocks. The concern for investors is that cheap, capable open-weight competitors undercut the spending assumptions behind the current AI capex boom.

title: Common Sense Media says Google’s AI Search poses an “unacceptable risk” to students. Google cannot turn it off.
blurb: Common Sense Media rated Google's AI Search an "unacceptable risk" for students, finding that AI Mode completed homework for minors and repeated misinformation authoritatively while running on Gemini models that are age-gated everywhere else in Google's products. Schools have no way to disable AI features without blocking Search entirely, and Google responded that the testing was "narrow and ambiguous" because it used consumer rather than education accounts.

title: Nebius raised $775 million by borrowing against its GPUs. It has $40 billion more contracts to securitise.
blurb: Nebius raised $775M in its first secured debt facility, borrowing against deployed GPU infrastructure and contracted cash flows at SOFR + 2.50%, maturing in 2030. MUFG led the oversubscribed nine-bank syndicate, and Nebius said it plans to repeat the structure against more than $40B in remaining contracted revenue from customers including Meta and Microsoft.

title: Google preparing Gemini Live and Skills for web rollout
blurb: Recent Gemini web builds contain a Gemini Live button and traces of skills being wired into regular chats, signaling that both features may move beyond their current limits. Live is currently mobile-only and in Trusted Tester testing, while skills are gated inside Gemini Spark on the AI Ultra plan. Neither has been announced.

title: Claude can now sign into websites with 1Password without exposing your credentials
blurb: 1Password released 1Password for Claude, a beta integration that lets Claude sign into websites during browser tasks without secrets entering its context window or Anthropic's infrastructure. Each credential request surfaces in the 1Password extension for biometric approval, is scoped to the current task, and triggers an Agentic Mode that hides the rest of the vault from the agent.

title: The EU’s AI Act starts requiring chatbot and deepfake labels on August 2 — while its tougher rules on hiring, biometrics and migration are pushed back to December 2027
blurb: The EU AI Act's transparency rules take effect on August 2, 2026, requiring companies across all 27 member states to disclose when users are interacting with a chatbot and to label deepfakes, AI-generated media, and AI-written articles on matters of public interest. A simplification law approved in late June delayed the high-risk obligations covering hiring, credit scoring, biometric identification, and border control to December 2027, with AI embedded in physical products pushed to August 2028.

title: China is rebuilding the smartphone around AI agents. ZTE’s NaviX sold out in hours.
blurb: ZTE's NaviX Ultra, an agentic AI phone running ByteDance's Doubao, sold out its 30,000-unit run at 3,499 yuan.

title: Grok's CLI caught uploading all your local files to the cloud
blurb: Developers found that xAI's Grok CLI uploaded local files to the cloud without disclosure, surfacing just as the tool gained traction on the back of the Grok 4.5 coding model. The same issue covers engineering leaders reporting rising code review load from AI-generated pull requests and enterprise developers pushing back on higher-than-expected pricing tiers.

title: OpenAI reduces Codex Model Context Size from 372k to 272k
blurb: OpenAI cut Codex's model context window from 372k to 272k tokens in a merged pull request.

title: OpenAI will start notifying parents if their teen has been kicked off of ChatGPT
blurb: OpenAI will now notify parents with linked teen accounts when their child's account is deactivated for violating policies on violent threats or acts of violence. The feature was built with Moonshot, a firm focused on countering online violence, and follows scrutiny over OpenAI's failure to alert authorities after banning the account of the 2025 Tumbler Ridge mass shooting suspect.

title: Homomorphically encrypted CIFAR-10 inference in 200ms
blurb: Belfort Labs demonstrated fully homomorphically encrypted CIFAR-10 inference running in 200ms, with the model never seeing plaintext inputs. FHE inference has historically been measured in seconds to minutes per image, so the latency drop is the claim worth checking against your own workload before treating encrypted inference as production-viable.

title: Sources: Kimi developer Moonshot told investors it is preparing for a Hong Kong IPO in as early as six months; its ARR hit $300M in June, up from $200M in April (Bloomberg)
blurb: Moonshot, the Chinese lab behind Kimi, told investors it is preparing a Hong Kong IPO as soon as six months out, with ARR at $300M in June, up from $200M in April. That is 50% growth in two months from a lab most Western founders still treat as a research curiosity rather than a commercial competitor. A listed Chinese frontier lab with public financials also gives the market its first clean read on what frontier model revenue and burn actually look like at that scale.

title: Moonshot Plans IPO in Six Months After China AI Breakthrough
blurb: Moonshot AI has told investors it plans to list publicly in as early as six months, moving fast after its latest model shifted how the market reads China's AI capabilities and knocked tech stocks lower. The timing is the story: the company is converting a single model release into public-market access before sentiment moves again. For founders raising against a China-capability narrative, the gap between frontier labs in the US and China is now being priced by public investors, not just benchmarked in papers.

title: Trump targets Brazil's payments system while dollar stablecoins are quietly overtaking country's payments
blurb: The US will impose a 25% Section 301 tariff on most Brazilian goods starting July 22, the first time that trade authority has been aimed at another country's domestic payment system. Washington's argument is that Pix, used by more than 90% of Brazilian adults and now processing more transactions than credit and debit cards combined, disadvantages Visa and Mastercard through free consumer transfers and capped merchant fees. The dollar is already winning that market by other means, with dollar-linked stablecoins accounting for around 90% of Brazil's crypto transactions.

title: Netflix CPTO on AI and the future of product and tech roles | Elizabeth Stone
blurb: Netflix's CPTO Elizabeth Stone says systems thinking is now the most important skill she hires for, above any specific craft depth. The constraint has moved from producing work to judging it, since AI floods teams with output and someone has to hold the line on quality and signal. Netflix treats AI fluency as a universal expectation rather than something senior people grow into, which changes what you write into every job description and every performance bar.

title: Prediction Markets Swell to 27% of Sports Bets During World Cup
blurb: Prediction markets took 27% of sports betting volume during the World Cup, growing far faster than traditional sportsbooks over the same stretch. Kalshi and its peers are no longer a regulatory curiosity on the edge of the gambling industry, they are taking share from it during its highest-volume event. The pattern worth watching is that an exchange model with no house position can undercut incumbents on price and still win liquidity, which is the same setup that reshaped equities trading.

title: What I learned selling 2,500 MIDI recorders: Hardware is not so hard
blurb: The founder behind Jamcorder sold 2,500 units of a piano-recording device and found that the hardware, the part everyone warns you about, was the smooth part. The hard part was software: roughly 200K lines across firmware, app, and manufacturing tooling, built over three years before LLMs. He kept the device deliberately simple, with one screw, one PCB, no power button, and no USB-C, and his conclusion is that hardware is as hard as you make it.

title: Moonshot plans to list in Hong Kong within six months at a $30 billion valuation
blurb: Moonshot AI told investors it plans to list in Hong Kong within six months at a valuation above $30B, three years after founding. ARR hit $300M in June, up from $200M in April, and last week's Kimi K3 release matched or beat Anthropic's Fable 5 and OpenAI's GPT-5.6 on several benchmarks. The listing sets the public-market benchmark for Chinese AI, and Moonshot is pricing K3 near Anthropic Sonnet levels rather than undercutting on price, which is the real signal for anyone competing on cost against Chinese open-weight models.

title: AI Chip Startup Etched Is in Talks for $20 Billion Valuation
blurb: Etched is in talks for a $20B valuation while simultaneously raising a separate Sequoia-led round at $10B, a doubling that hasn't been earned by shipping volume so much as by the market's appetite for anything that might undercut Nvidia. The company bet everything on transformer-only silicon, which pays off enormously if the architecture holds and strands the whole design if it doesn't. Two rounds at two prices in the same cycle is a signal about how fast late-stage AI hardware pricing is moving away from any fixed reference point.

title: Meta in Talks to Lease Computing Power to Anthropic in Potential $10 Billion Deal
blurb: Meta is in talks to lease computing power to Anthropic in a deal that could be worth around $10B. The arrangement would turn Meta's capex into a revenue line and put it in the same rental business as Microsoft, Amazon, and Google, while giving Anthropic capacity it cannot build fast enough on its own. Compute is now scarce enough that direct rivals will supply each other, which is the clearest signal yet that access to chips, not model quality, is the binding constraint on who ships next.

title: Sources: CIA operative Jonny Gannon spied on G42 to probe its China ties and helped the UAE gain expanded access to US AI chips by allaying US suspicions (Wall Street Journal)
blurb: A CIA operative embedded at G42 spied on the Emirati AI firm to assess its China ties, then helped clear the way for the UAE to buy more US AI chips. Chip export approvals now run through intelligence work, not just policy review, which means the compute supply behind Gulf data centers depends on national security judgments founders can't see or forecast. If your infrastructure roadmap assumes Middle East capacity keeps coming online, the gating factor is diplomatic trust, and that can reverse faster than any supply contract.

title: Sales Rep Annual Quotas in 2026: What High Performers Are Actually Carrying: $750k for SMB, $1.35m for Mid-Market, $2.25m for Enterprise
blurb: Top-quartile enterprise AEs are now carrying $2.25M annual quotas, with mid-market at $1.35M and SMB at $750K, according to ICONIQ's 2026 GTM benchmark survey of 150+ B2B and AI software companies. Attainment went up rather than down, with the best AI-native companies hitting 85% to 90% versus the traditional 65% to 75%, because AI in marketing and SDR motions is generating 10 to 11 points more lead-to-MQL conversion. Comp followed the work, with AE pay tied to net new recurring revenue jumping from 25% to 33% in a single year.

title: How the Trump administration shifted from a "light-touch" approach to AI policy to an interventionist stance that led to restrictions on top AI models in the US (Leo Schwartz/The Information)
blurb: The Trump administration opened with a light-touch approach to AI policy and has moved to an interventionist one, with restrictions now landing on which top AI models can be used in the US. That makes model availability a policy variable rather than a vendor one, and it applies to companies that never thought of themselves as regulated. If your product sits on a single frontier provider, the useful work now is knowing which models the rules cover and what the fallback looks like before a decision forces the answer.

title: Permit hurdles push up costs for AI data centers; Oracle pivoted from gas turbines to costlier fuel cells for its Project Jupiter in NM, costing billions more (Ann Davis Vaughan/The Information)
blurb: Permitting, not chips, is becoming the binding constraint on AI buildouts. Oracle abandoned gas turbines for fuel cells at its Project Jupiter site in New Mexico after running into permit hurdles, a swap that adds billions to the project's cost. The pattern matters for anyone whose unit economics depend on inference prices staying flat, because power procurement delays and expensive workarounds land in compute pricing eventually, and the labs reselling that capacity have no reason to absorb the difference themselves.

title: France and Germany pledge to build a European rival to Palantir’s military AI software
blurb: France and Germany signed a joint declaration pledging to build a European sovereign alternative to Palantir's military AI software, with France's Arcadia command-and-control platform as the starting point. Both countries' intelligence services already dropped Palantir for ChapsVision, and the Bundeswehr has excluded the company from its defence cloud procurement entirely. A NATO commander recently said no real European alternative to Palantir's Maven exists, which is the gap Paris and Berlin are now funding, and it opens a procurement lane for European defence software startups that state buyers previously routed around.

title: SpaceX in Talks to Provide Computing Power for Pentagon’s AI Push
blurb: SpaceX is in talks to sell the Pentagon billions of dollars' worth of data-center capacity for its AI programs, according to people familiar with the deal. A launch company becoming a compute supplier says as much about who can actually build power-hungry infrastructure right now as it does about defense procurement. For founders competing for GPU capacity, the constraint is shifting from chips to the land, power, and construction capability that a small set of firms already control.

title: Nebius raised $775 million by borrowing against its GPUs. It has $40 billion more contracts to securitise.
blurb: Nebius raised $775M in secured debt priced at SOFR + 2.50%, borrowing against deployed GPU clusters and the contracted cash flows from an investment-grade customer. The structure treats GPUs the way airlines treat aircraft, as collateral against long-term revenue contracts, and nine banks across the US, Europe, and Japan oversubscribed it. With more than $40B in remaining Meta and Microsoft commitments to securitise, Nebius can fund buildout without diluting shareholders, and every other compute provider with signed hyperscaler contracts now has a template for doing the same.

title: Corgi, a startup that insures other startups, doubled its valuation to $2.6 billion three weeks after its last round — and because the same investors backed both, the jump is drawing quiet scrutiny over “internal markups”
blurb: Corgi, which sells business insurance to startups, raised $106M at a $2.6B valuation three weeks after closing a $160M round at $1.3B, with much of the same investor group in both. When a fund participates in a step-up round it just priced, it marks up its own existing stake without any outside buyer or liquidity event setting the number. LPs are noticing, and the growing distrust of internal markups means founders should expect diligence on who actually set their last price, not just what it was.

title: The EU’s AI Act starts requiring chatbot and deepfake labels on August 2 — while its tougher rules on hiring, biometrics and migration are pushed back to December 2027
blurb: The EU AI Act's transparency rules take effect August 2, 2026, requiring companies to disclose chatbot interactions, mark generative AI output as machine-made, and label deepfakes and AI-written articles across all 27 member states. The high-risk obligations covering hiring, credit scoring, biometrics, and border control were pushed to December 2027 by a simplification law approved at the end of June, with AI in physical products moving to August 2028. If you sell into Europe, the near-term compliance work is labeling and disclosure, and you have another 18 months before the rules that actually constrain what you can build arrive.

title: Databricks raising new funding at $188B valuation
blurb:
Databricks is finalizing a round led by Coatue that adds $3B to its balance sheet at a $188B valuation. The money goes toward Genie, its AI assistant suite, plus Lakebase and Unity AI Gateway, a governance layer that polices agent output and flags opportunities to cut agent hardware costs. CEO Ali Ghodsi framed the shift as enterprises moving from tokenmaxxing to valuemaxxing, wanting the best outcome per dollar rather than the smartest model on every task. Part of the proceeds is earmarked for acquisitions, following the $1B purchase that became Lakebase.

Flag: the source contradicts itself. Headline says $188B, body says $118 billion. I used the headline figure — verify before publishing.

title: Topcoat: The full full-stack framework for Rust
blurb: Topcoat is a server-rendered fullstack Rust framework from the Tokio team that lets async components query the database directly, removing the separate API layer. Its `$(...)` expressions are type-checked Rust that compile to both server-side render code and JavaScript, so client interactivity runs without a wasm bundle or client build step. The project is early-stage and expects breaking changes.

title: Setting up your spare Mac for Claude Code to control, a step-by-step guide
blurb: The guide walks through configuring a spare Mac as an always-on host for Claude Code with computer use enabled, reachable from the Claude mobile app or over SSH from a main machine. The setup trades container isolation for full macOS access, letting the agent drive Mac-only apps like Unity and run with `--dangerously-skip-permissions` on hardware that has nothing worth losing.

title: Claude Code uses Bun written in Rust now
blurb: Claude Code v2.1.181 and later ship the Rust port of Bun, which Jarred Sumner says made startup 10% faster on Linux with no other user-visible change. The author confirmed it locally by running strings on the Claude binary, which reports Bun v1.4.0 and lists 563 Rust source paths including src/bundler/bundle_v2.rs, a version that only exists in Bun canary.

title: Transcribe.cpp
blurb: Transcribe.cpp is a ggml-based speech-to-text library covering 16 ASR families and 60+ models, with acceleration via Vulkan, Metal, CUDA, and TinyBLAS. The author, who maintains the Handy desktop app, built it as a near drop-in whisper.cpp replacement after hitting the limits of ONNX's CPU-only performance, and every model ships numerically validated and WER tested against its reference implementation.

title: Regressive JPEGs
blurb: The author abused JPEG's progressive mode, where a file is split into scans that each fill in a spectral range, to concatenate multiple same-resolution images into one file that animates as it downloads over a slow connection. The trick caps out fast because most decoders stop after roughly nine scans as a zip-bomb guard, and baseline single-scan frames fail since progressive scans cannot mix DC and AC coefficients.

title: The dual-write bug you probably have in production (and the outbox pattern that fixes it)
blurb: The author walks through a Go implementation of the transactional outbox pattern, where domain events are written to the same Postgres transaction as the state change and a separate relay process publishes them to Kafka. The post argues events belong on the aggregate rather than the service layer, and covers why retry loops around a save-then-publish sequence shrink the failure window without ever making the two writes atomic.

title: Static search trees: 40x faster than binary search (2024)
blurb: The author implements a static B+ tree over sorted 32-bit integers and optimizes it to roughly 40x the throughput of binary search, reporting results in ns per query. The post works through manual SIMD node search, batching and prefetching to hide memory latency, pointer arithmetic that removes instructions from the inner loop, and prefix partitioning to cut cache misses on the upper levels, with all benchmark code in the repo.

title: Why your Cursor rules never fire: globs, alwaysApply, and description explained
blurb: The author explains why `.cursor/rules` files silently never attach: each of Cursor's four rule types is gated by a different frontmatter field, and a rule with no `alwaysApply`, `globs`, or `description` quietly defaults to Manual. The three failure modes are writing `globs` as a YAML array or quoted string instead of a bare comma-separated one, using `.md` instead of `.mdc`, and omitting the description that Agent-Requested rules match against.

title: The Database Is Not the Data Model
blurb: The author argues that schema design and data modeling are different disciplines, and that the industry collapsed them because DDL was visible, executable, and committable while conceptual models were not. The piece traces the drift from Codd's data independence and Chen's ER modeling through ORMs shipping `models.py` and dbt calling a SELECT statement a model. The result is that "model" now means an ERD, a table definition, a SQL transform, and a transformer, which leaves teams arguing about implementation before they have agreed on what the entities mean.

title: Databricks raising new funding at $188B valuation
blurb:
Databricks is finalizing a $3B round led by Coatue at a $188B valuation, with proceeds earmarked for AI capabilities and further acquisitions. Spending priorities are the Genie assistant suite, Lakebase, and Unity AI Gateway, a governance layer for agent quality and cost, which pushes Databricks further into competition with Snowflake over the agent and governance layer sitting above the lakehouse.

Flag, Oliver: the source contradicts itself. Headline says $188B, first body line says $118 billion. Worth confirming before this ships.

title: KPF wraps China skyscraper in one-kilometer-long "landscape ribbon"
blurb: KPF designed TP-Link LXD in Shenzhen, a 124,600-square-metre campus of three interconnected towers wrapped by a one-kilometre ribbon of terraces and planted staircases. The continuous circulation route turns the compact site's limited ground plane into stacked shared green space, threading offices, R&D floors, and housing together rather than stacking them as separate volumes.

title: Cities aren't malls. So why are they starting to look like them?
blurb: The authors argue that civic wayfinding has fallen into "mall-ification," where signage systems in Ohio, Texas, and Los Angeles read as interchangeable because they optimize for function alone. They point to Nicolas Vrignaud's work at Docks en Seine and Bruce Mau's Toronto Carpet Factory identity as counterexamples, where material, scale, and form are drawn from the site's own history rather than applied on top of it.

title: VulnHunter: Capital One's agentic AI code security tool
blurb: Capital One open-sourced VulnHunter, an agentic AI code security tool that analyzes source from an attacker perspective to identify exploitable defects, map attack paths, and propose targeted remediations. It runs an agentic reasoning workflow rather than pattern-matching like a traditional SAST scanner, and is built to sit in the developer workflow to cut triage burden on findings before they reach production.

title: CISA urges immediate SharePoint hardening as exploits mount
blurb: CISA added three actively exploited Microsoft SharePoint Server flaws to the KEV catalog, including CVE-2026-56164, an elevation-of-privilege bug that is remotely exploitable without authentication despite its 5.3 CVSS score. Microsoft has shipped updates for supported versions and recommends enabling AMSI integration, but CISA notes patching alone may not evict attacker persistence, so defenders should hunt for IOCs and rotate SharePoint machine keys.

title: TP-Link Kasa cameras leaked home GPS via unauthenticated UDP for 6 years
blurb: TP-Link Kasa EC71 cameras responded to unauthenticated UDP probes on the local network with device details including the owner's GPS coordinates, a flaw that shipped in the firmware for roughly six years before disclosure. Any attacker on the same network segment, or an app with local network permission, could geolocate a household without credentials. TP-Link has not published a fix, so segment the cameras onto an isolated VLAN.

title: Two new high severity WordPress vulnerabilities, patch immediately!
blurb: WordPress patched two SQL injection flaws, CVE-2026-60137 and CVE-2026-63030, the latter a REST API batch-route confusion issue that chains to remote code execution on 6.9 and the 7.1 beta. Fixes ship in 6.9.5, 6.8.6, and 7.1 beta2; sites that cannot update immediately should block anonymous access to /wp-json/batch/v1 and ?rest_route=/batch/v1 at the WAF as a temporary measure.

title: Claude can now sign into websites with 1Password without exposing your credentials
blurb: 1Password released 1Password for Claude, a beta integration that lets Claude Desktop on macOS complete authenticated browser tasks without secrets entering the model's context window or Anthropic's infrastructure. Credentials are filled through a 1Password-managed channel gated on biometric approval per task, and a new Agentic Mode locks the browser extension down to the approved credential while hiding the rest of the vault from the agent.

title: Microsoft warns of surge in ACR Stealer attacks on customers
blurb: Microsoft observed a surge in ACR Stealer infections against enterprise customers between late April and mid-June, with ClickFix lures delivering the payload via WebDAV shares and MSHTA. The malware-as-a-service operation, a suspected Amatera Stealer rebrand, decrypts browser credentials through DPAPI and exfiltrates session tokens, so defenders should block rundll32 and mshta execution from WebDAV paths and rotate exposed browser-stored credentials.

title: WordPress Core "wp2shell" RCE flaws get public exploits, patch now
blurb: Public proof-of-concept exploits are out for wp2shell, a chain of CVE-2026-63030 (REST API batch-route confusion) and CVE-2026-60137 (SQL injection in WP_Query's author__not_in) that gives pre-auth RCE on stock WordPress 6.9.0-6.9.4 and 7.0.0-7.0.1. WordPress forced auto-updates to 7.0.2 and 6.9.5; sites with auto-update disabled should patch and hunt for webshells now.

title: Update now: 7-Zip fixes RCE flaw exploitable with malicious archives
blurb: 7-Zip 26.02 patches a heap-based buffer overflow in XZ decompression that allows arbitrary code execution when a user opens a crafted archive. There are no reports of active exploitation, but 7-Zip has no auto-update mechanism, so defenders should push the build from 7-zip.org manually and watch for phishing campaigns delivering malicious archives.

title: Shark vacuums with flawed Amazon policy can easily expose millions of user data
blurb: A researcher found that Shark's Amazon Alexa skill integration uses an overly permissive account-linking policy, letting an attacker who knows a target's email enumerate and access device and account data across millions of vacuum owners. No patch has shipped, so operators with Shark devices on managed networks should unlink the Alexa skill and treat associated account credentials as exposed.

title: Qubes OS Security in the Public Record
blurb: A longitudinal analysis of 109 Qubes Security Bulletins (2011-2025) and the project's Xen Security Advisory tracker found that 87 of 109 QSBs (79.8%) trace to Xen, CPU/microarchitectural, or other upstream components rather than Qubes-core logic, with 113 of 464 XSAs affecting Qubes. Post-2018 annual disclosure rates are statistically flat, and S-shaped vulnerability discovery models did not beat a rolling-mean baseline on short-horizon forecasts. The measurable risk in compartmentalized architectures sits in the upstream trust anchors, not the isolation layer.

title: Grok's CLI caught uploading all your local files to the cloud
blurb: The Grok CLI was caught uploading local files from developer machines to xAI's cloud, surfacing just as the capable Grok 4.5 coding model was driving adoption of the tool. Teams that installed it should treat any secrets, .env files, and source in reachable directories as exposed, rotate credentials, and block the binary until xAI documents what was collected and how long it is retained.

title: Apple @ Work: New macOS ClickFix malware brings a new potential backdoor to your enterprise fleet
blurb: Netskope Threat Labs documented a macOS ClickFix campaign that uses fake optimization utilities, spoofed GitHub repos, and localized IT support pages to trick users into pasting a Terminal command that loads an AppleScript stealer entirely in memory. The payload then spoofs a System Preferences password prompt to unlock the keychain and exfiltrate saved credentials and session cookies, so defenders should alert on clipboard-sourced Terminal execution and treat affected keychains as compromised.

title: Ransomware curdles production at Coca-Cola's Fairlife dairy biz
blurb: A ransomware attack halted production at Fairlife, the Coca-Cola-owned dairy processor, disrupting operations at its manufacturing plants. Food and beverage manufacturers stay attractive targets because OT downtime is expensive and perishable inventory leaves little room to negotiate, so segmenting plant networks from IT and rehearsing manual fallback procedures matters more than backup coverage alone.

title: Microsoft warns of surge in ACR Stealer attacks on customers
blurb: Microsoft reported a surge in ACR Stealer infections across its enterprise customers between late April and mid-June, with attackers using ClickFix lures, WebDAV shares, and MSHTA to deliver a malware-as-a-service payload that harvests browser passwords, session cookies, and Microsoft 365 documents. Stolen authentication tokens bypass MFA outright, so affected tenants need session revocation and credential rotation, not just endpoint cleanup.

title: Microsoft confirms WSUS service degradation impacting all versions of Windows
blurb: Microsoft confirmed a service degradation in Windows Server Update Services affecting all supported Windows versions, disrupting the patch-distribution path many enterprises still rely on for managed update rollouts. Admins running on-premises WSUS rather than Intune or Autopatch face delayed security updates until Microsoft ships a fix, which strengthens the case for the cloud update management Microsoft has been steering customers toward.

title: TP-Link Kasa cameras leaked home GPS via unauthenticated UDP for 6 years
blurb: Security researchers found that TP-Link Kasa EC71 cameras broadcast their owners' GPS coordinates over an unauthenticated UDP service, an exposure that went unpatched for roughly six years. IT teams running consumer-grade cameras on corporate or hybrid-work networks should treat the finding as a reason to audit TP-Link firmware versions and segment IoT devices off routable VLANs.

title: WordPress Core "wp2shell" RCE flaws get public exploits, patch now
blurb: Public proof-of-concept exploits are now available for "wp2shell," a chain of two WordPress Core flaws (CVE-2026-63030 and CVE-2026-60137) that gives unauthenticated attackers remote code execution on stock installs running 6.9.0-6.9.4 and 7.0.0-7.0.1. WordPress has forced auto-updates to 7.0.2 and 6.9.5, but IT teams running self-hosted or version-pinned instances need to patch manually across an estimated 500M sites.

title: Update now: 7-Zip fixes RCE flaw exploitable with malicious archives
blurb: 7-Zip released version 26.02 to patch a heap-based buffer overflow in its XZ decompression path that lets a malicious archive execute arbitrary code as the opening user. 7-Zip ships with no auto-update mechanism, so IT teams need to push 26.02 manually across endpoints, and prior 7-Zip and WinRAR archive flaws were weaponized in phishing campaigns within months of disclosure.

title: Ransomware curdles production at Coca-Cola's Fairlife dairy biz
blurb: A ransomware attack disrupted production at Fairlife, the dairy subsidiary Coca-Cola acquired outright in 2020, taking plant operations offline at a manufacturer where downtime spoils perishable inventory rather than merely delaying it. Subsidiaries remain the soft entry point in large vendor stacks, and IT leaders running multi-brand environments should expect auditors to focus on segmentation between corporate systems and plant-floor OT.

title: Two new high severity WordPress vulnerabilities, patch immediately!
blurb: WordPress patched two vulnerabilities in 6.9.5 and 6.8.6, including CVE-2026-63030, a REST API batch-route confusion and SQL injection flaw that allows remote code execution on 6.9 installs. Teams that cannot patch immediately can block anonymous access to /wp-json/batch/v1 at the WAF level, though Searchlight Cyber warns this may break legitimate traffic and should stay temporary.

title: Shipyard: How We Built Slack's Next-Generation EC2 Platform
blurb: Slack's infrastructure team detailed Shipyard, the EC2 platform that replaced continuous Chef convergence on long-lived instances with immutable, deployable artifacts and progressive rollouts. The team hit the limits of configuration management across tens of thousands of instances, where drift was inevitable and service-level deploys required coordinating changes across multiple layers. Shipyard pushes that responsibility into build pipelines and automated safety checks, giving EC2 workloads that could not containerize the same rollout guarantees as app delivery.

title: The dual-write bug you probably have in production (and the outbox pattern that fixes it)
blurb: The author walks through the transactional outbox pattern in Go, arguing that saving to Postgres and then publishing to Kafka has no atomicity and silently drifts state when a process dies between the two calls. The fix is to write the event into the same database transaction as the state change and let a separate relay process publish it. The post covers the full implementation, including having the aggregate emit its own domain events rather than the service layer.

title: Netflix CPTO on AI and the future of product and tech roles | Elizabeth Stone
blurb: Netflix CPTO Elizabeth Stone says systems thinking has replaced functional depth as the skill she screens for, and that AI fluency is now expected at every level rather than treated as a senior-track differentiator. Her more immediate problem is triage: AI-generated output has multiplied faster than the review capacity to judge it, so the constraint has shifted from producing work to deciding which work is worth keeping.

title: You're doing "Activation" wrong
blurb: The author argues the standard activation rate, users hitting an aha milestone divided by signups, has stopped working as a growth metric because AI-driven acquisition brings in more users but not the right ones. Activation is a company problem rather than a growth-team problem, and teams that optimize the ratio in isolation end up trading long-term efficiency for short-term wins. The piece draws on the author's work as CPGO at Fyxer, where curating strategy and ICP documentation for AI tooling still failed to make the real problem areas obvious.

title: Prediction Markets Swell to 27% of Sports Bets During World Cup
blurb: Prediction markets accounted for 27% of sports betting volume during the World Cup, growing far faster than traditional sportsbooks over the same stretch. The shift puts venues like Kalshi in direct competition with licensed sportsbooks on their highest-volume event of the cycle, without the state-by-state gaming licenses those operators carry.

title: Trump targets Brazil's payments system while dollar stablecoins are quietly overtaking country's payments
blurb: The US will impose a 25% Section 301 tariff on most Brazilian goods starting July 22, the first use of that authority to target a country's domestic payment system, citing Pix rules that cap merchant fees and mandate free service for individuals. Dollar-linked stablecoins already account for roughly 90% of Brazil's crypto transactions, meaning dollar settlement is spreading through the market the tariff is meant to defend.

title: eToro Takes Strategic Stake in Onchain Derivatives Exchange Extended, Plans Zengo Tie-Up
blurb: eToro took a strategic stake in Extended, a Starknet-based perpetual futures decentralized exchange, as part of a $12.5M round reported by CoinDesk, and plans to pair the venue with Zengo, the self-custody wallet it acquired in April for roughly $70M. The tie-up would let eToro offer onchain perpetuals across Extended's 100-plus crypto, equities, FX, and commodities markets to wallet users who retain custody of their assets.

title: France orders country's internet service providers to block Polymarket
blurb: France's gambling regulator, the ANJ, ordered internet service providers to block Polymarket on July 16, classifying the prediction market as an illegal gambling site rather than a financial trading venue. The order follows a November 2024 ban on financial transactions that users bypassed with VPNs, with Polymarket drawing 205,057 unique French visitors in June, and puts France among more than 30 countries restricting the platform.

title: Tether's USDT hits 2-year countdown threatening its position on U.S. crypto platforms
blurb: The GENIUS Act passed its one-year mark on Saturday, starting a two-year countdown for Tether to bring USDT into compliance or lose access to US platforms by July 2028. Tether's latest disclosures show roughly a quarter of USDT reserves sit in assets that fail GENIUS standards, while US-based rival Circle has moved to pre-comply and federal regulators have missed their own rulemaking deadline.

title: Inside Zcash's new node that targets Visa-scale privacy at 50,000 transactions per second
blurb: Zcash developers released Zakura 1.0.0, an independently maintained full node forked from the Foundation's Zebra client, adding pruning, fast sync, and legacy zcashd compatibility ahead of that client's July 18 end of life. Zakura supports the Ironwood (NU6.3) upgrade activating July 28, which adds a turnstile capping Orchard shielded pool withdrawals to contain any counterfeit ZEC from a long-standing soundness bug.

title: Kraken says simpler options can unlock crypto's next derivatives market
blurb: Kraken launched European-style, USD-settled bitcoin and ether options on Kraken Pro, initially via request-for-quote for eligible clients outside Europe, North America, and Australia. The exchange's derivatives director argues product design rather than demand has kept crypto options a small share of trading volume against incumbents Deribit, CME, and Binance, and plans to add an order book, more assets, and a European rollout.

title: Here is why a massive $1.6 billion in crypto liquidity is sitting idle and wasting away
blurb: Dune research commissioned by 1inch found that $1.6B of the $1.84B tracked across concentrated liquidity pools on Uniswap, PancakeSwap, and Aerodrome was underutilized in H1 2026, with $542M sitting fully out of range in an average week. Out-of-range positions forgo roughly $150M in annual fees, though rebalancing carries its own transaction costs and execution risk.

title: France doubles down on restricting access to Polymarket
blurb: France's gambling regulator ANJ ordered ISPs to block Polymarket after finding French residents logged 578,751 visits in June despite a November 2024 ban on transactions from the country. Advertising an unlicensed betting site now carries fines up to €100,000, and the action follows Spain blocking both Polymarket and Kalshi and Minnesota banning prediction markets outright.

title: Bitcoin’s quantum problem gets a recovery tool, but not for Satoshi’s 1.1 million coins
blurb: Project Eleven published a zero-knowledge proof scheme that lets a wallet's key-derivation path serve as ownership evidence after quantum computers can forge ECDSA signatures, running in 243ms on a laptop. The design targets BIP-361, the Lopp proposal that would block deposits to quantum-vulnerable addresses after three years and freeze them after five, stranding over 34% of supply. The prototype is unaudited and incomplete, and Satoshi's ~1.1M BTC sit in addresses too old to carry the hashed derivation material the proof requires.

title: The time for Clarity is here: The next-generation Telecoms Act
blurb: Former Congressman Patrick McHenry argues in Fortune that the Clarity Act, the crypto market structure bill moving through Congress, is the most consequential technology legislation since the Telecommunications Act of 1996. He counters the position that existing securities law is sufficient, pointing to bipartisan backing across multiple committees and to crypto businesses that are asking for defined boundaries rather than no regulation at all.

title: Visa Is Expanding Its Crypto Push With New Stablecoin Platform
blurb:
Visa announced a new stablecoin platform, extending a crypto push that already includes USDC settlement pilots on Solana and Ethereum. The launch positions Visa as infrastructure for bank-issued stablecoins rather than a competitor to them, giving regulated issuers distribution across Visa's existing merchant network.

(46 words. Flagging: the Context field was empty, so I wrote from the headline plus Visa's documented USDC settlement history. If the Bloomberg piece has specifics — launch partners, chains, volume figures — those belong in sentence two instead of my framing.)

title: Tether's USDT hits 2-year countdown threatening its position on U.S. crypto platforms
blurb: The GENIUS Act passed its first anniversary with no federal financial regulator having finished implementing rules, starting a two-year countdown that Tether must clear by July 2028 to keep USDT on U.S. platforms. Tether's latest disclosures show as much as a quarter of USDT reserves sit in assets that fail GENIUS standards, while U.S.-based Circle has spent the year pre-complying.

title: Prediction Markets Swell to 27% of Sports Bets During World Cup
blurb: Prediction markets captured 27% of US sports-betting volume during the World Cup, growing far faster than traditional sportsbooks over the same stretch. Kalshi and its peers operate as CFTC-regulated exchanges rather than state-licensed books, which lets them take national volume without 50-state licensing and leaves incumbents like DraftKings and FanDuel competing against a lighter regulatory cost base.

title: South Korea Details Plan for Foreigners to Trade Won Easier
blurb: South Korea published a detailed plan to let foreigners trade the won freely, its furthest move yet toward full convertibility after decades of tightly controlled onshore FX access. Offshore market makers and payment providers would gain direct won liquidity outside Seoul trading hours, cutting hedging costs and settlement friction for cross-border flows into a market that still routes most non-deliverable forward volume through offshore proxies.

title: eToro Takes Strategic Stake in Onchain Derivatives Exchange Extended, Plans Zengo Tie-Up
blurb: eToro became a strategic investor in Extended, a Starknet-based onchain perpetuals exchange, in a round CoinDesk pegged at $12.5M, and paired it with Zengo, the self-custody wallet eToro bought in April for roughly $70M. Combining Zengo's custody with Extended's cross-asset derivatives engine lets eToro offer onchain perps to users who keep control of their assets, mirroring Robinhood's push into onchain product suites.

title: France orders country's internet service providers to block Polymarket
blurb: France's gambling regulator, the ANJ, ordered internet service providers to block Polymarket on July 16, classifying the prediction market as an illegal gambling site rather than a financial trading venue. The order follows a November 2024 ban on financial transactions that users bypassed with VPNs, with Polymarket drawing 205,057 unique French visitors in June, and puts France among 30-plus countries restricting the platform.

title: Trump targets Brazil's payments system while dollar stablecoins are quietly overtaking country's payments
blurb: The US will impose a 25% Section 301 tariff on most Brazilian goods starting July 22, the first time the trade authority has been aimed at a foreign country's domestic payment system. USTR argues Pix's free-for-consumers mandate and capped merchant fees disadvantage Visa and Mastercard in a market where the instant-payment rail now clears more transactions than cards, even as dollar stablecoins already make up roughly 90% of Brazil's crypto volume.

title: Visa Is Expanding Its Crypto Push With New Stablecoin Platform
blurb: Visa is building a new stablecoin platform that lets banks and fintechs issue and settle their own tokens on top of Visa's existing network, extending a crypto push that already moves USDC across four blockchains. The move puts Visa in direct competition with the stablecoin infrastructure providers its issuer clients would otherwise use, and positions the card network as a settlement layer rather than a bystander to it.

title: Singapore Eyes Hedge Fund Tax Cuts to Boost Hub Role: FT
blurb: The Monetary Authority of Singapore has held talks with investment firms about cutting taxes for fund managers, part of an effort to keep the city-state competitive as an asset-management hub and retain talent, per an FT report. The move reads as a response to competition from Hong Kong and Dubai, both of which have courted fund managers with favorable tax treatment over the past two years.
